original_hypothesis.title: Underlying wrapper insolvency via fee-on-transfer or deflationary underlying: minting 1:1 to requested amount can undercollateralize LiquidityGauge
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: depositUnderlying mints gauge shares equal to the requested _amount, not the actual underlying received. If the underlying token is fee-on-transfer/deflationary (or otherwise transfers less than requested), the gauge will mint more shares than assets held, causing eventual withdrawal failures and loss for later withdrawers.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While docs claim non-standard tokens are out-of-scope, production deployments often encounter token upgrades or edge behaviors. If governance accidentally whitelists such a token, the gauge becomes economically unsafe immediately.
deployment_assumptions: A LiquidityGauge is deployed for an underlying token that charges transfer fees / burns on transfer or otherwise transfers less than _amount
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No deployment-time validation exists in LendingLedger.whiteListLendingMarket or LiquidityGauge constructor to enforce standard-transfer semantics; no allowlist of safe tokens is visible in code.

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeTransferFrom(_user, this, _amount); _mint(_user, _amount); no check of actual received amount (balanceBefore/After)

preconditions: LiquidityGauge deployed for a token with non-1:1 transfer semantics
At least one deposit occurs (creating mismatch between totalSupply and underlying balance)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Wrapper token assumes ERC20 transfer amount equals requested amount, which is false for fee-on-transfer tokens
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Governance must whitelist/deploy gauge for such a token (or underlying token behavior changes via upgrade)

operational_questions: Can the underlying tokens be upgraded to fee-on-transfer after gauge deployment?
Is there a governance process that verifies token semantics before whitelisting?
Are there any existing markets whose underlying has transfer hooks/fees?
validation_requirements: Simulate with a fee-on-transfer ERC20: verify undercollateralization and eventual withdrawal failures
Inspect whitelisted markets/token implementations for non-standard transfer behavior
Add monitoring: totalSupply vs underlying balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deposit underlying that transfers less than _amount to the gauge
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Receive full _amount gauge shares despite lower collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Withdraw underlying later; early withdrawers may drain available collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Late withdrawers revert or receive less, realizing losses

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Insolvency of wrapper; withdrawal failures and loss of user funds
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Deposits/withdrawals and reward share accounting tied to gauge balances

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: WithdrawUnderlying reverts due to insufficient underlying balance
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Deposits into gauges with known non-standard tokens

validation_result.impact_summary: If a LiquidityGauge is deployed for a fee-on-transfer or deflationary underlying token, more gauge shares are minted than underlying received, leading to insolvency and withdrawal failures for later users.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The depositUnderlying function in LiquidityGauge.sol mints gauge shares equal to the requested `_amount` parameter without verifying the actual amount of underlying tokens received by the contract. For fee-on-transfer tokens, `safeTransferFrom(user, this, _amount)` delivers less than `_amount` to the contract, but `_mint(user, _amount)` creates the full `_amount` of gauge shares. This creates an immediate undercollateralization: totalSupply() > IERC20(underlyingToken).balanceOf(address(this)). When users later call withdrawUnderlying, which attempts `safeTransfer(user, _amount)`, the last withdrawers will revert because the contract lacks sufficient underlying balance. The standard mitigation is to measure `balanceOf(address(this))` before and after the transfer and mint only the difference.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 36-40 of LiquidityGauge.sol clearly show the defect pattern: safeTransferFrom followed by _mint with the same _amount, with no balance check. No validation of token transfer semantics exists in whiteListLendingMarket (LendingLedger.sol lines 153-165) or the LiquidityGauge constructor (lines 21-30).

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Mints _amount gauge tokens regardless of how many underlying tokens were actually received. For fee-on-transfer tokens, actual received < _amount, causing undercollateralization.
Attempts to transfer _amount underlying tokens back, but the contract may not hold enough due to undercollateralization from deposits, causing revert for later withdrawers.
No validation of the underlying token's transfer semantics when deploying a LiquidityGauge. Any ERC20 address is accepted without checking for fee-on-transfer behavior.

calling_sequence: 2. IERC20(underlyingToken).safeTransferFrom(user, address(this), _amount) transfers tokens - for fee-on-transfer tokens, actual received = _amount - fee
3. _mint(user, _amount) mints the full _amount of gauge shares, not the actual received amount
4. _afterTokenTransfer triggers sync_ledger(user, +int256(_amount)) on LendingLedger, recording _amount in the ledger
6. Later, withdrawUnderlying(_amount) calls safeTransfer(user, _amount) which reverts if insufficient balance
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is at LiquidityGauge.sol:37-38. safeTransferFrom at line 37 may transfer less than _amount to the contract (fee-on-transfer), but _mint at line 38 always creates exactly _amount gauge tokens. The standard fix is: uint256 balBefore = IERC20(underlyingToken).balanceOf(address(this)); safeTransferFrom(...); uint256 received = IERC20(underlyingToken).balanceOf(address(this)) - balBefore; _mint(user, received);

deployment_assumptions_checked: A LiquidityGauge is deployed for an underlying token that charges transfer fees / burns on transfer or otherwise transfers less than _amount
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deploy.sol only deploys VotingEscrow, GaugeController, and LendingLedger. Market whitelisting and gauge deployment happen post-deployment via governance calling whiteListLendingMarket. No scripts validate token transfer semantics. The code at LendingLedger.sol:159 deploys gauges for any address passed by governance with no token behavior checks.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is straightforward: (1) Governance whitelists a market with a fee-on-transfer underlying token and enables gauge. (2) User A deposits 1000 tokens; 50 tokens are taken as fee; gauge receives 950 but mints 1000 shares. (3) User B deposits 1000 tokens; fee takes 50; gauge receives 950 more (total 1900 underlying, 2000 shares). (4) User A withdraws 1000 underlying successfully (1900-1000=900 remaining). (5) User B tries to withdraw 1000 but only 900 underlying exists - transaction reverts. User B loses 50 tokens permanently. This also poisons the LendingLedger accounting since sync_ledger records the inflated amounts.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If deployed with a fee-on-transfer token: (1) LiquidityGauge becomes undercollateralized proportional to cumulative transfer fees. (2) Last withdrawers cannot retrieve their underlying tokens. (3) LendingLedger reward accounting is also inflated, potentially allowing early depositors to claim more CANTO rewards than deserved. (4) No recovery mechanism exists in the contracts.
recommended_mitigations: Use balance-before/after pattern in depositUnderlying: measure actual tokens received and mint only that amount
Add token validation in whiteListLendingMarket to reject known fee-on-transfer tokens or document the restriction prominently
Consider adding a sweep/rescue function for stuck tokens in case of misconfiguration
Apply same pattern in withdrawUnderlying for tokens that may charge fees on outgoing transfers
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a MockFeeOnTransferERC20 with 5% transfer fee. 2. Deploy LendingLedger and whitelist the mock token with _hasGauge=true. 3. Have User A approve and call depositUnderlying(1000e18). 4. Assert totalSupply() == 1000e18 but IERC20(underlying).balanceOf(gauge) == 950e18. 5. Have User B do the same. 6. User A calls withdrawUnderlying(1000e18) - succeeds. 7. User B calls withdrawUnderlying(1000e18) - reverts due to insufficient balance.
uncertainties: Whether any currently whitelisted markets on mainnet use fee-on-transfer tokens (requires on-chain investigation)
Whether any planned underlying tokens could be upgraded to add transfer fees in the future
The exact fee percentage of any such tokens affects the severity of undercollateralization
additional_testing_needed: Enumerate all currently whitelisted markets and verify their underlying token transfer semantics
Test with rebasing tokens as well, which could cause similar but different collateralization issues
Verify whether the LendingLedger reward inflation from phantom deposits creates additional extractable value
